unitLabel: Units III & IV — Magnetic Effects & Magnetism
title: Magnetic Properties of Materials
summary: MaterialMagnetic susceptibility $ \chi…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - The incorrect relation is $ 1 < \mu_r < 1 + \varepsilon $ For diamagnetic material, magnetic susceptibility is small and negative i.e., $-1 \leq \chi \leq 0$.
##### Caution Students should not get confused between relative permeability and magnetic susceptibility of diamagnetic, paramagnetic and ferromagnetic materials. -
MaterialMagnetic susceptibility $ \chi $
Diamagneticslightly less than 0
Paramagneticslightly greater than 0
Ferromagnetic$ \gg 1 $
Non-magnetic0
- The magnetic moment of a diamagnetic atom is equal to zero. - Energy of current source will be converted into potential energy of the rod. - Magnetic susceptibility = $ \chi $ It is negative for diamagnetic material only. - (I) Paramagnetic material will be feebly attracted, (II) Diamagnetic material will be feebly repelled, (III) Ferromagnetic material will be strongly attracted (IV) Non-Magnetic material will remain unaffected by magnetic field. - If a diamagnetic substance is brought near the north or the south pole of a bar magnet, it is repelled by both the poles. - Curie temperature is that temperature above which a ferromagnetic material becomes paramagnetic material. - Above curie temperature, ferromagnetic material behaves as paramagnetic material. - $ \mu_p \neq 0 $, $ \mu_f \neq 0 $ and $ \mu_d = 0 $ - A diamagnetic material in a magnetic field moves from stronger to the weaker part of the field.
##### Related Theory A diamagnetic material tends to move from stronger to weaker regions of the magnetic field and hence decreases the number of magnetic field lines passing through it. - From Curie Law, $ \chi_{m} \propto \frac{1}{T} $ - Material's magnetic susceptibility is a measurement of how easily a specimen of that material may be magnetized in a magnetic field. Magnetic susceptibility ( $ \chi_{m} $) of a diamagnetic substance is temperature independent. - When diamagnetic substances are placed in the magnetic field of a powerful magnet, they are weakly magnetized in the opposite direction of the field and repelled by the powerful magnet.
##### Related Theory Relative permeability of the diamagnetic substance is negative.

unitLabel: Units III & IV — Magnetic Effects & Magnetism
title: Objective I
summary: **Hysteresis**: when a ferromagnetic material is subjected to a cyclic magnetic field, the magnetisation $M$ does not retrace the same path during increasing and decreasing field.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Magnetic field lines: - **Outside** the magnet: leave from the N-pole and enter the S-pole (N to S in external space). - **Inside** the magnet: run from S to N (completing the closed loop). They form **continuous closed loops** — unlike electric field lines which start and end on charges. Answer: (B).
- In a **uniform** field $\vec{B}$: - The forces on the two poles ($+qm$ and $-qm$) are equal and opposite → **no net force**. - But the two forces form a couple if the dipole is not aligned → **net torque** $\tau = MB\sin\theta$. In a **non-uniform** field, there is also a net translational force. Answer: (C).
- $\vec{\tau} = \vec{M}\times\vec{B}$, so $\tau = MB\sin\theta$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{M}$ and $\vec{B}$. The dipole experiences a restoring torque that aligns it with the field. At $\theta = 0°$: $\tau = 0$ (stable equilibrium); at $\theta = 90°$: $\tau = MB$ (maximum). Answer: (C).
- $U = -\vec{M}\cdot\vec{B} = -MB\cos\theta$. - At $\theta = 0°$ (aligned with field): $U = -MB$ (minimum, stable equilibrium). - At $\theta = 180°$ (anti-aligned): $U = +MB$ (maximum, unstable equilibrium). - At $\theta = 90°$: $U = 0$. Answer: (B).
- Magnetic moment $M = IA$ (current × area) has SI unit $\rm A\cdot m^2$. Alternatively, since $U = -MB\cos\theta$ and $[U] = \rm J$, $[B] = \rm T$: $$[M] = \frac{[U]}{[B]} = \frac{\rm J}{\rm T} = \rm J/T$$ Both $\rm A\,m^2$ and $\rm J/T$ are equivalent. Answer: (D).
- $$\oint_S \vec{B}\cdot d\vec{A} = 0$$ Unlike electric charges, **magnetic monopoles** do not exist (no isolated N or S poles have been found). Every magnet has both poles; magnetic field lines form closed loops. Therefore the net flux through any closed surface is zero. Answer: (C).
- **Diamagnetic materials** (e.g., bismuth, copper, water) have a small **negative** susceptibility ($\chi < 0$). When placed in a non-uniform field, the induced magnetisation opposes the field. The net force on a diamagnetic material is toward **weaker field** regions — i.e., it is **weakly repelled** from regions of strong field. Answer: (C).
- Below the Curie temperature $T_C$, ferromagnetic materials have long-range magnetic order (aligned domains). Above $T_C$, thermal energy destroys the alignment: - The material becomes **paramagnetic** ($\chi$ small positive, follows Curie–Weiss law $\chi = C/(T - T_C)$). - It does not become diamagnetic or superconducting. Answer: (B).
- **Curie's law** for paramagnetic materials: $$\chi = \frac{C}{T} \propto \frac{1}{T}$$ where $C$ is the Curie constant. At higher temperatures, thermal agitation randomises the magnetic dipoles more effectively, reducing the net magnetisation for a given applied field. Answer: (C).
- For any magnetic material: $\mu_r = 1 + \chi$. - **Diamagnetic**: $\chi < 0$ (small negative) $\implies \mu_r < 1$ (slightly less than 1). - **Paramagnetic**: $\chi > 0$ (small positive) $\implies \mu_r > 1$ (slightly greater than 1). - **Ferromagnetic**: $\chi \gg 1 \implies \mu_r \gg 1$ (much greater than 1, up to $10^5$). Answer: (B).
- **Hysteresis**: when a ferromagnetic material is subjected to a cyclic magnetic field, the magnetisation $M$ does not retrace the same path during increasing and decreasing field. The magnetisation **lags behind** the applied field $H$ — this is hysteresis. The area of the hysteresis loop represents the energy dissipated as heat per cycle. Retentivity (residual $M$ at $H=0$) and coercivity ($H$ needed to demagnetise) characterise the loop. Answer: (B).

unitLabel: Units III & IV — Magnetic Effects & Magnetism
title: Objective II
summary: - **A** ✓: Cutting a magnet never isolates a pole — each piece becomes a complete dipole.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: Unlike electric field lines, magnetic field lines always form closed loops (since there are no magnetic monopoles). - **B** ✓: If two field lines crossed, the field would have two directions at that point — impossible. - **C** ✓: By convention, outside the magnet, lines go N→S. - **D** ✓: Closer lines → stronger field (analogous to electric field lines). Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- - **A** ✓: Cutting a magnet never isolates a pole — each piece becomes a complete dipole. - **B** ✓: $M = m \cdot l$ (pole strength × length). Length halved, pole strength unchanged → $M' = m\cdot(l/2) = M/2$. ✓ - **C** ✓: Pole strength $m$ is an intrinsic property of the poles; cutting perpendicular to the length doesn't affect them. ✓ - **D** ✗: Magnetic monopoles don't exist; each piece has both poles. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: Below $T_C$, domains align spontaneously → net magnetisation without external field. - **B** ✓: Domains are regions of uniform magnetisation; domain boundaries (walls) separate them. - **C** ✓: Above $T_C$, ferromagnets become paramagnetic (Curie–Weiss law). - **D** ✗: Ferromagnetics have **large positive** susceptibility ($\chi \gg 1$). Small negative $\chi$ describes diamagnetics. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: Paramagnetism → weak attraction to external fields (small positive $\chi$). - **B** ✓: $\chi > 0$ for paramagnets. - **C** ✗: For paramagnets, $\chi = C/T$ (Curie's law) → $\chi$ **decreases** with temperature. - **D** ✓: Aluminium, platinum, oxygen, sodium are paramagnetic. Answer: (A), (B), (D).

unitLabel: Units III & IV — Magnetic Effects & Magnetism
title: Overview
summary: Given, each magnet has magnetic dipole moment = $m$.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- Given, each magnet has magnetic dipole moment = $m$. $\therefore$ Net magnetic moment $=\sqrt{m^{2}+m^{2}+2m^{2}\cos\theta}$ Here, $m_{\mathrm{net}}$ is max when $\cos\theta$ is max and $\cos\theta$ will be max when $\theta$ is minimum. So, $\theta = 30^{\circ}, m_{\mathrm{net}}$ will be maximum. 8.(B) The magnetic pole strength be m, then magnetic moment, $ M = ml $ From the given figure, $$ l=\frac{\pi}{3}\times r $$ $$ r=\frac{3l}{\pi} \tag*{...(i)}$$ ∴ New magnetic moment, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{M}&=m\times r=m\times\frac{3l}{\pi}\\&=\frac{3}{\pi}\times ml=\frac{3\mathb
- For AC source, Given. $$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}}=12\mathrm{~V},\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{rms}}=0.2\mathrm{~A} $$ Impedance, $$ Z=\frac{V_{rms}}{I_{rms}}=\frac{12}{0.2}=60\Omega $$ and for DC source, Given. $$ V=12V,I=0.4A $$ Resistance. $$ \mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{I}}=\frac{12}{0.4}=30\ \Omega $$ Since, Z > R, we conclude there must be another component in the series, which is inductor. ∴ It is a series LR circuit.
- From Curie Law, $ \chi_{m} \propto \frac{1}{T} $

unitLabel: Units III & IV — Magnetic Effects & Magnetism
title: The Bar Magnet
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - The magnetic potential energy when a magnetic dipole of magnetic moment $ \vec{m} $ is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field $ \vec{B} $ is given by $ U = -\vec{m} \cdot \vec{B} $ $ \therefore U = -mB \cos 90^\circ = 0 $ - Given, magnetic moment = $ 1 \times 10^{-2} $ Am $ ^{2} $ Moment of inertia, $ I = \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^{2}} $ kgm $ ^{2} $ No. of oscillations = 10 time $ t = 10 $ s $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{10}{10}=1\mathrm{~s} $$ $$ \mathrm{B}=\frac{1}{m}\left(\frac{2\pi}{\mathrm{T}}\right)^{2} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{B} &= \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^2} \left( \frac{2\pi}{\mathrm{T}} \right)^2 \\ &1 \times 10^{-2} \\ \mathrm{B} &= \frac{10^{-6}}{\pi^2 \times 10^{-2}} (2\pi)^2 \\ &= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-2} \pi^2} \times 4\pi^2 \\ \mathrm{B} &= 4 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{T} \end{aligned} $$ - $$ \mathbf{M}^{\prime}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{\boldsymbol{\theta}}{2\pi} $$ Given: M is magnetic moment of iron bar $$ \theta=60^{\circ} $$ $$ \mathbf{M}=\mathbf{M}\frac{60^{\circ}}{2\pi} $$ $$ 60^{\circ}=\frac{60\pi}{180}=\frac{\pi}{3}radians $$ So. $$ \mathbf{M}^{\prime}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{\overline{3}}{2\pi}=\mathbf{M}.\frac{1}{6} $$ - Given B = 0.049 T, $$ \mathrm{T}=\frac{5}{20}=\frac{1}{4},\mathrm{I}=9.8\times10^{-6}\mathrm{kg}\mathrm{m}^{2} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}=2\pi\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{I}}{m\mathrm{B}}} $$ $$ \mathrm{T}^{2}=\frac{4\pi^{2}\mathrm{I}}{m\mathrm{B}} $$ $$ m=\frac{4\pi^{2}\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{T}^{2}\mathrm{B}} $$ On substituting the given values in eqn (i), we get $$ \begin{aligned}m&=\frac{4\pi^{2}\times9.8\times10^{-6}}{\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}\times4.9\times10^{-2}}\\&=1280\pi^{2}\times10^{-5}Am^{2}\\x&=1280\pi^{2}\end{aligned} $$ -
Magnetic pole strength remains the same $$ M_{new}=m L_{eff}=\frac{mL}{2}=\frac{M}{2} $$ - Since work done in rotating the magnet, $$W = MB (\cos \theta_0 - \cos \theta)$$ where, $M = \text{magnetic moment of the magnet}$$ $$B = \text{magnetic field}$$ $$W = MB (\cos 0^\circ - \cos 60^\circ)$$ $$= MB \left( 1 - \frac{1}{2} \right) = \frac{MB}{2}$$ $$MB = 2W$$ $$ \begin{array}{c} putting\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}=2\mathbf{W} from(i),\\\tau=2\mathbf{W}\sin60^{\circ}\\=2\mathbf{W}\times\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\mathbf{W}\sqrt{3}\end{array} $$ - The magnetic pole strength be m, then magnetic moment, $ M = ml $ From the given figure, $$ l=\frac{\pi}{3}\times r $$ $$ r=\frac{3l}{\pi} $$ ∴ New magnetic moment, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{M}&=m\times r=m\times\frac{3l}{\pi}\\&=\frac{3}{\pi}\times ml=\frac{3\mathbf{M}}{\pi}\end{aligned} $$ - Here, $$ \begin{array}{c}B\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\end{array}\begin{array}{c}\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\longrightarrow\\\end{array} $$ $$ \tau=\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{M}}\times\overset{\rightarrow}{\mathbf{B}} $$ $$ \left|\tau\right|=MB\sin90^{\circ} $$ $$ \mathbf{F}l=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B} $$ $$ l=\frac{\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}}{\mathrm{F}} $$ - We know that, U = -MB cos θ for stable equilibrium, θ = 0° U = -MB cos 0° = -MB = -(0.4)(0.16) = -0.064 J - The force on stationary charge Q is zero. - According to the question; When magnetic dipole is rotated from initial position $ \theta_{1} $ to final position $ \theta_{2} $, then $$ \mathbf{W}=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}(\cos\theta_{1}-\cos\theta_{2}) $$ $$ \mathrm{W}=\mathrm{MB}\left(\cos0^{\circ}-\cos60^{\circ}\right) $$ $$ W=\mathbf{M}\mathbf{B}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{2\times10^{4}\times6\times10^{-4}}{2} $$ $$ \mathbf{W}=6\mathbf{J} $$ - A magnetic torque $ \tau $ acts on a bar magnet when it is placed in an external magnetic field B, which is given by, $$ \overrightarrow{\mathbf{r}}=\overrightarrow{\mathbf{M}}\times\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}=[\mathbf{M}]\left[\mathbf{B}\right]\sin\theta $$ where, $ \theta = \text{angle between M and B} $ - Given, $$ \chi=599 $$ Magnetic field, B = 1200 Am $ ^{-1} $ Permeability of the material of the rod. $$ \begin{aligned}\mu_{\mathrm{m}}&=\mu_{\mathrm{o}}\left(1+\chi\right)\\&=4\pi\times10^{-7}\left(1+599\right)\\&=2.4\pi\times10^{-4}\mathrm{~T~m A}^{-1}.\end{aligned} $$
